title: Surface-Adsorbed Nanodroplets of Symmetric Diblock Copolymers Form Versatile and Stimuli-Responsive Nanostructures
authors: Artem Petrov, Guillermo A. Hernández-Mendoza, Alfredo Alexander-Katz
abstract: Block copolymers often create droplets when placed on a substrate. Such nanostructured droplets can be arranged into regular microstructured arrays, thereby forming hierarchically organized materials that can be used in microelectronics, plasmonics, sensing, photonics, metamaterials production, and even cryptography. However, it is unclear if such materials can be stimuli-responsive, i.e., be able to change their nanostructure on a single droplet level upon applying external stimuli. In this work, we discovered that small (10-100 nm) surface-adsorbed droplets of symmetric diblock copolymers can form a multitude of different externally switchable nanostructures. We obtained a near-equilibrium, comprehensive 4D diagram of droplet morphologies by performing large-scale self-consistent field theory (SCFT) calculations under various wetting and phase separation conditions. The SCFT modeling was augmented with a computational algorithm that established an equilibrium droplet morphology in a given system without assuming potentially equilibrium structures prior to simulation. The discovered droplet nanostructures agreed excellently with previously published experimental data. Crucially, we showed that direct and reversible transitions between different droplet morphologies are possible upon changing the interaction strength between components, which can be tuned externally in experiments by adding surfactants or controlling temperature. We confirmed experimental realizability of such stimuli-responsiveness by modeling surfactant addition that led to a switch between droplet nanostructures. This work demonstrates that even the simplest symmetric diblock copolymers are able to produce versatile and stimuli-responsive structures on a surface when confined to a small nanodroplet. This opens the possibility to produce smart coatings with externally switchable hierarchical micro- and nanostructures.
summary: This study uses SCFT to map the equilibrium phase behavior of small block copolymer nanodroplets on surfaces. It demonstrates that these droplets can undergo reversible structural transitions in response to external stimuli, offering a pathway to creating smart, switchable hierarchical coatings.
main_problem: Understanding the structural versatility and stimuli-responsiveness of small (10-100 nm) surface-adsorbed symmetric diblock copolymer nanodroplets.
main_result: The discovery of a 4D morphological diagram showing that these droplets can form various switchable nanostructures, such as striped ellipsoids and onion-like particles, via external stimuli like temperature or surfactants.
method: Large-scale Self-Consistent Field Theory (SCFT) calculations in 2D and 3D, augmented by a custom Iterative Correction Algorithm (ICA) to establish near-equilibrium morphologies.
model_or_system: Small (10-100 nm) surface-adsorbed droplets of symmetric diblock copolymers (A and B blocks) in a surrounding medium (C) on a substrate modeled with tethered homopolymers (S).
key_observables: Droplet morphology/nanostructure classes, contact angle, surface preference, and free energy.
important_parameters: Flory-Huggins interaction parameters (chi_AB, chi_AC, etc.), contact angle (theta), and surface preference (Pref_A).
main_assumptions: The 3D calculations focused on nanodroplets and excluded certain film-like or micelle-like structures; the surfactant model assumed incompatibility between surfactant blocks and the primary BCP blocks.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the model of the surface and medium; Figures 2 and 3 present 2D morphological diagrams under different wetting conditions; Figure 4 shows 3D equilibrium structures and comparison with experimental AFM/SEM; Figure 5 illustrates the effect of surfactant addition.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of droplet versatility, describes the SCFT and ICA methodology, presents the 4D morphological phase diagrams, demonstrates stimuli-responsiveness through surfactant modeling, and validates results against experimental data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Sketch of the studied system. Symmetric diblock copolymers formed a droplet on the surface; A and B segments are shown in red and green, respectively. Surface was covered by the short brush S colored in light cyan. Melt of homopolymers C that modeled the surrounding medium (vacuum, gas, solvent or solvent vapor) is colored in dark blue. Before SCFT calculations, we prepared a homogeneous droplet (χAB = 0) having a certain contact angle θ (left). After this, χABN was set to a target value and a nanostructured droplet was formed (an example of such structure is shown on the right).

Figure 2: Equilibrium morphological diagram of nanodroplets adsorbed at a surface with no pref- erence towards any of the blocks (PrefA ≈0). SCFT calculations were performed in 2D space. Diagrams (a), (b), and (c) were obtained at θ ≈141◦, θ ≈92◦, and θ ≈67◦, respectively. Points of different colors represent the boundaries of different morphologies at a fixed χACN. Continuous lines between the boundary points show that this morphology was obtained at all values of χABN between these points. A characteristic snapshot for each structure as well as its short name are shown for each morphological region. A blocks are shown in red, B blocks are colored green, the surface-tethered S homopolymers...

Figure 3: Morphological diagram of nanodroplets adsorbed at a surface with "strong" preference towards A blocks (PrefA ≈0.157); the surrounding medium is either neutral or preferential to the same block (χAC ≤χBC). SCFT calculations were performed in 2D space. Diagrams (a), (b), and (c) were obtained at θ ≈141◦, θ ≈92◦, and θ ≈67◦, respectively. Points of different colors repre- sent the boundary structures of different morphologies at a fixed χACN. Continuous lines between the boundary points show that this morphology was obtained at all values of χABN between these points. For θ ≈141◦and small χABN ≈10, the striped ellipsoidal droplet transitioned into disk- like structure smoothly. We...

Figure 4: Equilibrium morphological diagram of three-dimensional nanodroplets adsorbed at a surface with no preference towards any of the blocks (PrefA ≈0). Diagrams (a), (b), and (c) were obtained at θ ≈141◦, θ ≈92◦, and θ ≈67◦, respectively. Points of different colors represent different morphologies. A characteristic snapshot for each structure as well as its short name are shown for each morphology. Smaller images located next to each structure show a slice of the morphology in the middle. A blocks are shown in red, B blocks are colored green, the surface- tethered S homopolymers are shown in light cyan, and homopolymers C modeling the surrounding medium are not shown. Experimental...

Figure 5: The change of equilibrium nanodroplet structure upon addition of short DE symmetric diblock copolymers that modeled amphiphilic surfactant molecules. A and B blocks are shown in red and green. Before the addition of surfactant, the surface only consisted of S homopolymers modeling the crosslinked random copolymer substrates used in experiment31 (light cyan). S was moderately incompatible with the surrounding medium modeled by homopolymer C (not shown). After the addition, DE diblock copolymers adsorbed at the surface forming a three-layer substrate (blocks D are shown in light orange, and blocks E are colored in violet).
